# Discussion
A central assumption underlying the use of graded truth ratings is that they provide a more fine-grained measure of perceived truth. The present findings call this assumption into question. Across a large collection of illusory truth effect (ITE) datasets, we found evidence that graded truth ratings capture more than perceived truth alone. Specifically, repetition increase certainty not only for statements judged as true, but also for statements judged as false. This pattern was observed both in datasets containing explicit certainty ratings and in graded truth ratings, where increased certainty manifested as responses moving farther away from the scale midpoint. 

Therefore, changes in graded truth ratings cannot necessarily be interpreted as changes in perceived truth. Instead, a graded truth rating may reflect a main effect on perceived truth, a main effect on certainty, and their interaction. Furthermore, the relative contribution of these components may vary across individuals, stimuli, and experimental contexts. Thus, graded truth ratings combine multiple psychological processes whose joint influence may not be apparent from the observed ratings alone.

Importantly, these differences in psychological meaning were not accompanied by substantial differences in effect size. Transforming response formats had little influence on the magnitude of the ITE, suggesting that the average repetition effect is relatively robust to the chosen response format. In contrast, reliability was consistently affected by response format. Although the overall difference between observed dichotomous and graded response formats was not statistically significant, transforming dichotomous judgments into graded responses using accompanying certainty ratings increased reliability, whereas dichotomizing graded ratings reduced reliability. Importantly, our analyses indicate that the additional information in the case of graded truth ratings does not merely represent a more precise measurement of perceived truth. Instead, graded ratings appear to combine multiple sources of systematic variance, including perceived truth, certainty, and their interaction. Consequently, a higher reliability should not automatically be interpreted as evidence that graded truth ratings provide a better measure of perceived truth. 

More generally, our findings suggest that separating truth judgments and certainty judgments may be advantageous whenever possible. Collecting a binary truth judgment alongside a separate certainty rating allows researchers to distinguish between changes in perceived truth and changes in confidence. At the same time, the two measures can be recombined when a more reliable composite measure is desired. Furthermore, separating these components also opens the door to cognitive modeling approaches developed for binary decision tasks (such as diffusion modeling; see e.g., @stump2026cognitive).

Additionally, our findings may be relevant to any psychological domain in which subjective judgments can be measured either dichotomously or on graded scales. Constructs such as belief, confidence, attitude strength, moral evaluation, and memory are often assessed using rating scales whose psychological interpretation is not always straightforward. The present results suggest that graded ratings may combine information about the judgment itself and the certainty with which it is held. Separating these components may therefore improve construct interpretation and facilitate the use of cognitive models that distinguish decisions from confidence across a wide range of psychological phenomena.

Our work also extends recent investigations of reliability in the illusory truth effect. Whereas previous research has focused primarily on graded truth judgments (@zajdler2025psychometrics), the present study provides one of the first systematic examinations of reliability in dichotomous ITE measures. By leveraging counterfactual transformations of existing datasets, we were able to isolate the contribution of response format to reliability. More broadly, our findings help reconcile recent reports of both high (@dekeersmaeckerTruthRepetitionReliably2025) and low (@calioAssessingTruthEffects2025a) ITE reliability by showing that reliability is not an inherent property of the effect itself, but varies across experiments and samples and depends on methodological factors such as response format, the number of statements, and the retention interval.

Finally, several limitations should be acknowledged. Most importantly, datasets containing both dichotomous truth judgments and separate certainty ratings were limited to a small number of studies. Consequently, further research is needed to establish the generality of these findings across different paradigms, populations, and response formats. Nevertheless, the present results suggest that response formats influence not only the reliability of truth judgments but also their psychological meaning. Understanding what different response formats measure is therefore a prerequisite for interpreting their psychometric properties and for drawing conclusions about how various manipulations, such as repetition, shape beliefs.
